sources.notes: Statement and constraints: count completions of a partially colored n by m grid such that the number of adjacent unequal-color pairs is even.
Luogu solution derives the key conclusion that only boundary non-corner cells affect the parity.
CSDN writeup explains the same parity invariant by considering how flipping different grid cells changes the number of unequal adjacent pairs.
Luogu problem page was used to cross-check constraints and the precolored-cell input format.

annotation.summary: Count the number of ways to color the unfilled cells of a partially colored grid so that the number of adjacent unequal pairs is even.
annotation.constraints: Multiple test cases with total k <= 2e5; n and m can be large, so the solution can inspect only the precolored cells and the four borders.
annotation.core_idea: View colors as 0/1 and the number of unequal adjacent pairs as the sum over grid edges of color_u xor color_v. Over GF(2), summing all incident edge terms cancels every even-degree vertex and leaves only vertices of odd degree. In a rectangular grid, corners have degree 2, interior cells degree 4, and boundary non-corner cells degree 3, so the required parity is exactly the xor/sum of colors on boundary non-corner cells. If at least one such key cell is uncolored, exactly half of all completions satisfy the parity condition: all other cells are arbitrary and the last free key cell is forced. Otherwise, the precolored key cells already determine whether the answer is all completions or zero.
annotation.complexity: O(k + log(nm)) time per test case for scanning fixed cells and fast exponentiation; O(1) extra memory besides input counters
tricks: Rewrite unequal adjacent pairs as edge xor terms and sum them modulo 2.
Only odd-degree vertices survive in the parity expression.
If a free variable appears in a single parity equation, exactly half of assignments satisfy it.

solution_variants.name: odd-degree-boundary-parity
solution_variants.summary: Count fixed and free boundary non-corner cells. If any key cell is free, answer 2^(nm-k-1); otherwise answer 2^(nm-k) or 0 depending on the fixed key-cell parity.

tags.evidence: The main observation is that the parity of unequal-color grid edges is a GF(2) cut parity equal to the sum of colors on odd-degree vertices.
tags.description: Use a graph cut or edge-disagreement parity identity to convert edge conditions into a parity expression over vertices, usually odd-degree vertices.
tags.created_reason: Existing graph and parity tags do not specifically identify the reusable cut-parity identity used to collapse all grid edges.

tags.evidence: The problem only asks whether the number of unequal adjacent pairs is even, and the solution works entirely modulo 2.
After reducing to one parity equation, the count is either 0, all 2^(free cells), or exactly half of them.
The final condition is a single linear equation over GF(2) on the colors of boundary non-corner cells.
The remaining work is counting binary assignments under a parity constraint.
